title: The net-zero house starter kit: retrofitting Indonesia's Type 36 home
summary: Why the cheapest, most-built house in Indonesia is the right place to start a net-zero conversation — a short summary of my final degree project.
body:
Most net-zero building research aims high — bespoke homes, premium envelopes, budgets the mass market will never reach. My final degree project at Kyushu University goes the other way: to the **Type 36**, the 36 m² "starter home" mass-produced by the thousand across Indonesia.

## The thermal oven

A standard Type 36 is built from uninsulated *batako* concrete-block walls and a thin zinc roof. In Jakarta's 29–31 °C climate, that envelope behaves like an oven: the roof radiates solar heat straight into the living space, and the walls store heat all day and release it all night. Households fight back with air conditioning set as low as 20 °C — pushing annual bills past **IDR 15 million** on a grid that is still around 61% coal.

## A "Cooling Survival Kit"

Instead of rebuilding the house, I tested a low-cost retrofit on the *existing* stock, simulated in Energy3D against Jakarta weather data:

- **Roof insulation** — glass wool, bringing the roof U-value down to 0.5 W/m²K
- **Shading** — window overhangs and trees
- **Inverter AC** — COP 4.0 instead of 2.8
- **A behavioural nudge** — raising the setpoint from 20 °C to 22 °C

## The result

> The combined kit cut simulated annual electricity use by ~57% — from 10,553 kWh to 4,521 kWh.

That drops annual energy cost from ~IDR 15.2M to ~IDR 6.5M. The inverter upgrade alone pays for itself in about three months; the full kit reaches a simple payback of roughly five years on measures that last decades.

## Why it matters

Indonesia's "One Million Houses" program prioritises quantity over thermal performance, locking millions of new households into decades of high energy dependence. A retrofit costing ~5% of construction is a scalable, decentralised contribution to the country's Net Zero 2060 goal — and it builds energy literacy along the way.

[Read the full paper on ResearchGate →](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/406981225_Abinael_Type_36_House_Paper_Final)

title: Can a local LLM build a real app? (FinTrack)
summary: Notes from FinTrack — handing the first build to a Gemma model running on local hardware, then hardening it with Claude.
body:
FinTrack started as a question, not a product: how far can a model running entirely on local hardware get on a real full-stack app?

## The setup

I ran a Gemma 4 26B a4b QAT model locally through **LM Studio**'s locally hosted server (my own macbook pro m2 max) (`127.0.0.1:1234`) and pointed **OpenCode** at it in build mode. It generated the Next.js app piece by piece — a theme toggle, a transaction form, smart income/expense categories, dark mode — narrating each change as it went.

## Where it broke

It got surprisingly far, and then it didn't. Mid-build the site collapsed into what I can only call recovery mode: bare HTML, no styling, no charts — "everything in basic mode now, no visualization." Watching a local model confidently rewrite files while the app quietly fell apart was the most instructive part of the whole experiment.

## The fix

I handed the broken codebase to Claude to review and harden. It restored the UI, fixed the multi-currency logic (IDR / USD / JPY with daily FX conversion), and brought back the trend charts and category donut. The experiment became something that actually runs.

## What I took away

- Local models can **scaffold** a real app shockingly fast.
- The gap shows up in **coherence** — holding a growing codebase together, catching edge cases, not breaking what already worked.
- Pairing a local model (cheap, private, fast first drafts) with a frontier model (review and hardening) is a genuinely useful workflow, not a gimmick.

title: Building a self-learning trading agent that experiments on its own
summary: I taught my AI assistant to run trading experiments 24/7 — change one variable, backtest, compare scores, keep or revert. Here's how the system works and what it's learned so far.
body:
I wanted to know: could I turn my AI assistant into something that doesn't just answer questions, but actually **experiments** on its own?

Most trading bots just execute. This one **learns**.

## The idea

Instead of writing analysis, my assistant runs experiments — the scientific method, one variable at a time, on a loop. It sleeps, I sleep, but the experiments keep going.

The system is built around a simple loop that runs every 4 hours:

> Pick a strategy → change one param → split into train/validation → backtest both → compare scores → KEEP or REVERT → log → repeat

The core engine is locked. The strategies are mutable. The journal persists everything. It's Karpathy-style minimalism applied to quantitative finance.

## The architecture

![Trading Agent Architecture](/research/trading-agent-architecture.png)

## What happened when I let it run

The system ran 3 experiments before hitting the safety gate:

1. **SMA_Crossover slow SMA 20 → 30**: REVERT. The validation score cratered from 0.035 to 0.0.
2. **SMA_Crossover fast SMA 5 → 10**: REVERT. Returns went negative, Sharpe collapsed.
3. **Same experiment again (a bug)**: REVERT. Streak hit 3 — auto-pause.

That bug is now fixed. The state persistence didn't track which experiment to run next, so it kept running the same one. Also fixed the IDX stock data gap threshold — Indonesia has way more holidays than the US, the 5% gap threshold was too strict. And silenced the noisy Yahoo Finance 404 errors for delisted pairs like BTC-IDR.

## What I learned

The safety mechanisms work — the 3-strike rule properly halts experimentation. The paper trading opens and tracks positions correctly. But the system is only as smart as its strategies, and right now those are basic — SMA crossovers, RSI reversals, Bollinger breakouts. Nothing revolutionary.

What's interesting is watching it run without me. It's like having a junior quant who never sleeps, doesn't complain, and documents every single experiment.

## What's next

Now it's running again with a clean streak, testing SMA fast 5 → 15. The cron jobs handle the 24/7 schedule:

- Every 2h: market scan + paper trades
- Every 4h: one experiment
- 07:30 JST: morning briefing
- 20:00 JST: daily report to Obsidian

I want to see how many experiments it takes before something actually works. The system is patient. So am I.

---

*You can find the formal research entry on this site, the full source on [GitHub](https://github.com/abinaelsl/trading-agent), or the experiment journal in the Trading section of my Obsidian vault.*
